title: What Is Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?
content:
Phase II archaeological evaluation is a critical step in the cultural resource management (CRM) process, determining whether an archaeological site qualifies for listing on the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP). When federal undertakings or federally funded projects intersect with previously identified sites, Phase II evaluation provides the data agencies need to make informed compliance decisions under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act.

Quick Answer: Phase II archaeological evaluation is a targeted subsurface investigation that tests whether a site identified during Phase I survey meets National Register of Historic Places eligibility criteria. Archaeologists excavate systematic test units, recover artifacts, and analyze site integrity and significance to give agencies the information needed for Section 106 compliance decisions.

Unlike the broad reconnaissance of a Phase I survey, Phase II work focuses intensively on a specific site or set of sites already flagged as potentially significant. The findings directly shape project planning — a site determined eligible may require avoidance or historic preservation mitigation, while an ineligible site can be cleared for construction without further archaeological oversight.

Understanding how Phase II evaluation works helps federal agencies, state transportation departments, and private developers anticipate timelines, costs, and regulatory obligations before breaking ground.

Why Is Phase II Archaeological Evaluation Required?

Phase II evaluation is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies a site that cannot be immediately dismissed as insignificant. Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act requires federal agencies to assess effects on historic properties before approving or funding undertakings, and the NRHP eligibility determination is the gateway to that assessment.

The Advisory Council on Historic Preservation's regulations at 36 CFR Part 800 outline a step-by-step process. After Phase I identifies sites, the lead agency — in consultation with the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) — must determine whether additional investigation is needed to assess eligibility. Phase II provides that evidence.

For project proponents, a clear eligibility determination is valuable regardless of outcome. A finding of ineligibility removes the site from further Section 106 consideration, streamlining project approvals. A finding of eligibility gives all parties a defined basis for avoidance planning or mitigation agreements. Skipping or shortcutting Phase II creates legal and regulatory exposure that can delay or halt projects entirely.

What Methods Are Used in Phase II Evaluation?

Phase II evaluation relies on systematic subsurface excavation to characterize site content, boundaries, integrity, and potential research significance. The specific methods vary by site type, soil conditions, and regulatory context, but several techniques are standard across the Southeast.

Shovel test units intensify coverage within the site boundary to map artifact distribution and identify high-density areas worth closer examination. Hand-excavated test units — typically 1×1 meter or 1×2 meter squares — are then opened in targeted locations to expose stratigraphic profiles, document feature presence, and collect systematic artifact samples. All sediment is typically screened through ¼-inch mesh to maximize artifact recovery.

When sites contain features such as hearths, storage pits, or structural remains, GIS and remote sensing tools including ground-penetrating radar or magnetometry can guide unit placement and reduce unnecessary excavation. Recovered materials undergo laboratory analysis for artifact type, frequency, and temporal associations, while radiocarbon dating or other chronometric techniques may be applied to organic materials. The integrated dataset supports the written NRHP eligibility argument submitted to the SHPO and federal agency.

How Is NRHP Eligibility Determined During Phase II?

NRHP eligibility rests on two tests: a site must fall under at least one of four significance criteria (Criteria A–D), and it must retain sufficient integrity — the physical characteristics that convey its significance — to communicate that significance today.

In CRM archaeology, the overwhelming majority of sites are evaluated under Criterion D, which recognizes properties that have yielded or are likely to yield information important to prehistory or history. Phase II data — artifact assemblages, feature densities, stratigraphic context — directly address whether a site contains the research potential Criterion D requires.

Integrity is assessed across seven attributes: location, design, setting, materials, workmanship, feeling, and association. For buried archaeological sites, integrity of location and materials are most critical. A site that has been heavily disturbed by plowing, grading, or root activity may lack the stratigraphic integrity needed to answer meaningful research questions, supporting a finding of ineligibility even if artifacts are present.

The Phase II report synthesizes all findings into a formal eligibility recommendation, which the SHPO and lead federal agency review and must concur on before the Section 106 process can advance.

How Long Does Phase II Evaluation Take?

Phase II timelines depend on site size, complexity, the number of sites being evaluated, and laboratory processing demands. A straightforward single-component prehistoric site on a small project footprint might require two to four weeks of fieldwork and another four to eight weeks for laboratory analysis, report production, and agency review. Larger or more complex sites — multi-component occupations, sites with extensive feature sets, or historic period sites requiring archival research — commonly take three to six months from fieldwork to final approved report.

SHPO review adds additional time that varies by state and workload. In Alabama and across the Southeast, review periods of 30 to 60 days are typical, though complex cases or requests for additional data can extend this. Project proponents should build Phase II time into schedules well before construction deadlines, and coordinate early with the lead federal agency and SHPO to align on scope and expectations before fieldwork begins.

What Happens After Phase II Evaluation?

Once the SHPO and federal agency concur on an eligibility determination, the Section 106 process moves to the next step. If a site is found not eligible, no further archaeological work is typically required for that site, and the project can proceed in that area. If a site is found eligible, the agency must assess the project's effect on the historic property.

An "adverse effect" finding — meaning the project will diminish the qualities that make the site significant — usually leads to a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) outlining mitigation measures. The most common mitigation is Phase III data recovery, a large-scale excavation designed to retrieve the research information the site contains before it is destroyed. Other mitigation strategies may include project redesign to avoid the site, archaeological monitoring during construction, or preparation of a Historic American Landscapes Survey.

Understanding the full Section 106 sequence helps project teams plan budgets and schedules that account for all possible outcomes, reducing the risk of costly surprises during construction.

Frequently Asked Questions

What triggers a Phase II archaeological evaluation?

Phase II evaluation is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies an archaeological site that cannot be immediately determined insignificant, and when the lead federal agency and SHPO agree that additional investigation is needed to assess the site's National Register of Historic Places eligibility under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act.

How is Phase II different from Phase I archaeological survey?

Phase I survey covers a broad project area to identify any archaeological sites present, using widely spaced shovel tests. Phase II evaluation focuses intensively on one or more specific sites already identified, using systematic test unit excavation, artifact analysis, and feature documentation to determine National Register eligibility.

Who reviews Phase II archaeological reports?

Phase II reports are reviewed by the lead federal agency and the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO). Both parties must formally concur on the eligibility determination before the Section 106 process can continue. For projects on tribal lands or affecting tribal interests, Tribal Historic Preservation Offices (THPOs) are also consulting parties.

Can a project proceed if a site is found eligible after Phase II?

Yes. An eligible site does not automatically stop a project. The Section 106 process requires the agency to assess the project's effect on the eligible site and, if adverse effects are found, to consult with the SHPO and other parties on mitigation measures — typically a Phase III data recovery excavation or project redesign to avoid the site.

Plan Your Phase II Evaluation with Confidence

Phase II archaeological evaluation is a technically demanding process with significant regulatory stakes. A well-executed study provides the clear eligibility determination that agencies, SHPOs, and project proponents need to move forward — whether that means clearing a site from further consideration or defining a mitigation path.

excerpt: Phase II archaeological evaluation determines whether a site is eligible for the National Register of Historic Places. Learn how subsurface testing, artifact analysis, and site significance assessment guide CRM decisions.
